title: BSA: The Bloch Sphere Approach as a Geometrical Design Tool for Building Cost-Effective Quantum Gates
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"fdrm","text":"The Bloch sphere is a geometrical 3D sphere that visualizes the states of a qubit after a series of quantum gates are applied to it. In quantum computing, the Bloch sphere is mainly used as a geometrical visualization (and verification) tool.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":192,"length":49}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"4mk1","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"408sh","text":"On the other hand, in this protocol, we introduce the Bloch sphere as a geometrical design tool for building cost-effective quantum gates based on their rotational quantum operations in the XY, XZ, and/or YZ planes, which are the 2D circular projections along the axes of the Bloch sphere. Collectively, the Bloch sphere and its projectional planes are termed the Bloch sphere approach (BSA).","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":72,"length":23},{"style":"bold","offset":364,"length":27}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"710v0","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"6l3fr","text":"With the BSA, various generic and cost-effective quantum gates and libraries are designed for IBM quantum computers, using the symmetrical and semi-symmetrical structures [1-5], Clifford+T gates, and IBM native \"basis\" gates ( ,  ,  , and  ). Our designed generic and cost-effective quantum gates and libraries are listed as follows, where 2   n   5 5]","type":"ordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"italic","offset":25,"length":1}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"b11nt","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"7sgos","text":"Because the quantum operations of all IBM native gates mainly rotate around the X-axis and Z-axis of the Bloch sphere, the XY-plane of the Bloch sphere is utilized here for the BSA. However, the BSA can also be utilized to build generic and cost-effective quantum gates for other quantum computers, e.g., Intel, Google, and Rigetti, using other projectional planes of the Bloch sphere, e.g., the XZ and YZ planes, based on the supported native gates for such quantum computers. Therefore, in this protocol, we introduce the BSA as a generic and open geometrical framework for prospective quantum computing research and building interesting and innovative quantum gates and circuits.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{"0":{"type":"tex_formula","mutability":"MUTABLE","data":{"formula":"\\sqrt{X}","guid":"","id":0}},"1":{"type":"tex_formula","mutability":"MUTABLE","data":{"formula":"X","guid":"","id":0}},"2":{"type":"tex_formula","mutability":"MUTABLE","data":{"formula":"RZ","guid":"","id":0}},"3":{"type":"tex_formula","mutability":"MUTABLE","data":{"formula":"CNOT","guid":"","id":0}},"4":{"type":"tex_formula","mutability":"MUTABLE","data":{"formula":"\\le","guid":"","id":0}},"5":{"type":"tex_formula","mutability":"MUTABLE","data":{"formula":"\\le","guid":"","id":0}},"6":{"type":"tex_formula","mutability":"MUTABLE","data":{"formula":"\\sqrt{X}","guid":"","id":0}},"7":{"type":"tex_formula","mutability":"MUTABLE","data":{"formula":"CV","guid":"","id":0}},"8":{"type":"tex_formula","mutability":"MUTABLE","data":{"formula":"\\sqrt{X}^†","guid":"","id":0}},"9":{"type":"tex_formula","mutability":"MUTABLE","data":{"formula":"CV^†","guid":"","id":0}}}}
steps: The Bloch sphere consists of three axes (X-axis in blue, Y-axis in red, and Z-axis in green) with their corresponding rotational angles (θ X , θ Y , and θ Z ), where "+θ" denotes a counterclockwise rotational angle and "−θ" denotes a clockwise rotational angle, in radians.

The XY-plane of the Bloch sphere is divided into segments to represent the rotational quantum operations of Clifford+T gates and IBM native gates around the Z-axis as follows, where the black dots represent these segments as shown below. The semicircle segment is half of the XY-plane, to represent the quantum rotations of Z gates. Such that, the XY-plane has two semicircles. The quadrant segment is one-fourth of the XY-plane, to represent the quantum rotations of S and S^† gates. Such that, the XY-plane has four quadrants. The octant segment is one-eighth of the XY-plane, to represent the quantum rotations of T and T^† gates. Such that, the XY-plane has eight octants. The XY-plane visualizes the rotational quantum operations of Cifford+T gates \{Z,~S,~S^†,~T,~T^†\} around the Z-axis of the Bloch sphere, when a qubit is initially set to the |+\rangle state.

The Clifford+T gates are \{I,~X,~Y,~Z,~H,\sqrt{X},\sqrt{X}^†,~S,~S^†,~T,~T^†,~CNOT~(CX),~CY,~CZ,~SWAP\} . For the BSA, based on IBM native gates, we limit the Clifford+T gates to \{X,~Z,~H,\sqrt{X},~S,~S^†,~T,~T^†,~CNOT\} , which is denoted by CTG 0 . The gates of CTG 0 rotating around the X-axis of the Bloch sphere are \{X,\sqrt{X},~CNOT\} . The gates of CTG 0 rotating around the Z-axis of the Bloch sphere are \{Z,~S,~S^†,~T,~T^†\} . The H gate of CTG 0 is primarily used in the BSA, to transform the state of a qubit from the Z-axis ( |0\rangle or |1\rangle ) to the XY-plane ( |+\rangle or |-\rangle ), respectively, and vice versa. In general, the H , \sqrt{X} , and \sqrt{X}^† can be used as superposition gates. However, in the BSA, different geometrical analyses should be taken into account when using \sqrt{X} and \sqrt{X}^† as superposition gates to build cost-effective gates for various quantum computers. For n \ge 2 qubits, an n -bit quantum gate has n −1 controls (input qubits) and one target (output qubit), except for the SWAP and Fredkin (CSWAP) gates. In the BSA, all controls are connected to the target using CNOT gates, and the target has a defined number of CTG 0 gates \{H,~X,\sqrt{X},~Z,~S,~S^†,~T,~T^†\} . Every CNOT gate flips the target's state in the perimeter of the XY-plane by +~\pi radians, i.e., around the X-axis of the Bloch sphere, when its control is in the |1\rangle state. When all controls are connected to the target using CNOT gates, this allows to map the target among the controls, and an n -bit quantum gate is then cost-effectively fitted into the layout (architecture) of any quantum computer, e.g., IBM, Intel, Google, and Rigetti. Hence, SWAP gates are never utilized to connect the non-neighboring physical qubits of a quantum computer.
If the target is not controlling other qubits, then CTG 0 is limited to the set: CTG 1 = \{H,\sqrt{X},~X,~Z,~S,~S^†,~T,~T^†\} . Otherwise, CTG 1 = CTG 0 .
For the target, define all Clifford+T gates (based on IBM native gates) as the set: CTG 0 = \{H,\sqrt{X},~X,~Z,~S,~S^†,~T,~T^†,~CNOT\} .
For the target, define all segments of the XY-plane as the set: SEG 0 = { semicircles , quadrants , octants }.

For symmetrical and semi-symmetrical structures [1-5], transform the target's state of an n -bit quantum gate from the Z-axis of the Bloch sphere into the XY-plane using one H gate, where n \ge 2 qubits. Note that the target is initially set to either |0\rangle or |1\rangle state, depending on the operational purpose of such a quantum gate.

Since SEG 0 only identifies quantum gates rotating around the Z-axis of the Bloch sphere, then CTG 1 is limited to the set: CTG 2 = \{Z,~S,~S^†,~T,~T^†\} . Here, we assume that the 1-bit quantum rotations around the X-axis of the Bloch sphere are not required for the target. Otherwise, this step is negligible and CTG 2 = CTG 1 , but this will affect the selection of quantum rotations in the next steps!
Let nCNOT counts the total number of CNOT gates from the controls to the target, and the new sets of CTG and SEG will be defined as follows.
If nCNOT~=~1 , then CTG 3 = \{S,~S^†,~T,~T^†\} , and SEG 1 = { quadrants , octants }.
If nCNOT~=~2 , then a set of IBM native RZ gates defines CTG 3 = {RZ(θ 1 ), RZ(θ 2 ), ...}, where θ's \le \pm~\frac{\pi}{~3~} for arbitrary SEG 1 .
If nCNOT~=~3 , then CTG 3 = \{T,~T^†\} , and SEG 1 = { octants }.
If nCNOT~>~3 , then a set of IBM native RZ gates defines CTG 3 = {RZ(θ 1 ), RZ(θ 2 ), ...}, where θ's \le \pm~\frac{\pi}{~nCNOT~+~1~} for arbitrary SEG 1 .
Re-transform the final reached target's state of an n -bit quantum gate from the XY-plane into the Z-axis of the Bloch sphere using another H gate.

The nCNOT and permutative gates of CTG 3 define the desirable quantum operation (logic and behavior) of an n -bit quantum gate, which can be designed and visualized based on the SEG 1 of the XY-plane.

Similarly, the XZ-plane of the Bloch sphere is divided into segments (semicircles, quadrants, and octants) to represent the rotational quantum operations based on the native gates of a quantum computer, as illustrated below where the black dots represent the segments (semicircles, quadrants, and octants). The XZ-plane visualizes the rotational quantum operations of native gates for a quantum computer around the Y-axis of the Bloch sphere, when a qubit is initially set to the |0\rangle state.

Similarly, the YZ-plane of the Bloch sphere is divided into segments (semicircles, quadrants, and octants) to represent the rotational quantum operations based on the native gates of a quantum computer, as demonstrated below where the black dots represent the segments (semicircles, quadrants, and octants). The YZ-plane visualizes the rotational quantum operations of native gates for a quantum computer around the X-axis of the Bloch sphere, when a qubit is initially set to the |0\rangle state.

In general, the Bloch sphere approach (BSA) is a generic and open geometrical framework for building cost-effective quantum gates, based on the Clifford+T gates and the supported native gates of any quantum computer, e.g., IBM, Intel, Google, and Rigetti. For a quantum computer, various projectional planes ( P ) of the Bloch sphere can be combined and employed, depending on the operational purpose of a quantum gate that needs to be geometrically designed. The following figures depict arbitrary P around the utilized axes of the Bloch sphere, where the black dots represent the segments (semicircles, quadrants, and octants). Arbitrary m projectional planes ( P ) around the X-axis of the Bloch sphere, where m \ge 1. Note the YZ-plane Arbitrary m projectional planes ( P ) around the Y-axis of the Bloch sphere, where m \ge 1. Note the XZ-plane Arbitrary m projectional planes ( P ) around the Z-axis of the Bloch sphere, where m \ge 1. Note the XY-plane Arbitrary m projectional planes ( P ) around all three axes of the Bloch sphere, where m \ge 1.

Finally, the re-transformed target's state is considered the output of such a quantum gate. For instance, the target in the |1\rangle state indicates a solution ( True ), while its |0\rangle state indicates a non-solution ( False ).

The BSA geometrically builds cost-effective n -bit quantum gates of symmetrical and semi-symmetrical structures, by assigning appropriate angles to their rotational gates using the projectional planes of the Bloch sphere, where n \ge 2. The following figures demonstrate arbitrary n -bit symmetrical quantum gates, where: SP 1 and SP 2 are the superposition gates: H , \sqrt{X} , and/or \sqrt{X}^† . θ's are the Clifford+T gates \{Z,~S,~S^†,~T,~T^†\} and/or the rotational angles of IBM native RZ( \pm θ) gates. Note that θ's can have similar, semi-similar, or non-similar values (as rotational quantum gates). The 2-bit symmetrical quantum gate. The 3-bit symmetrical quantum gate. The 4-bit symmetrical quantum gate. The 4-bit symmetrical quantum gate.
